Q1: I tried to set up a consistent study routine, but honestly it was hard with limited resources at home. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time watching the course videos and making diagrams and mind maps. I usually aimed for about 11 hours a week, spreading it across evenings after my other responsibilities. Some weeks were more structured than others depending on what was due.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I wanted to be more regular, but when you're dealing with financial stress and limited resources, sometimes you just have to prioritize survival over perfect study schedules. Closer to deadlines, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks I tried to keep a steady pace of watching lectures and reviewing notes.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was new video content posted or when an assignment deadline was approaching. I also checked in to see discussion posts because interacting with classmates helped me feel less alone in struggling with the material. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even when I was scared about my performance.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, logging in became almost automatic for me, like checking my phone in the morning. I made it a habit to open the LMS right after dinner every night. The discussion forums especially pulled me back because I'd get notifications about replies. It became part of how I structured my evenings.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most useful for me since I learn best by seeing things explained visually. I watched over 200 minutes of content and would pause to draw out concepts. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused. Those peer interactions kept me going when I felt scared about failing.
Q6: I'll be honest, I sometimes ignored the reading materials because they were dense and I didn't have money to print them out, so reading on screen for hours hurt my eyes. Some of the supplementary resources felt overwhelming when I was already struggling to keep up with the core content. I knew I should engage with them more, but time and energy were limited.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to what would be on the exam or in assignments. Visual content like videos and diagrams were always worth it to me. If something helped me understand a concept I'd been struggling with, that feeling of clarity made the effort worthwhile. Discussion posts felt valuable when classmates actually responded thoughtfully.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford when I was already behind on required work. Required activities had this weight of consequence attached to them, so I prioritized those even when I was exhausted. Sometimes I wished I had time for optional stuff because it looked interesting, but with my assignment completion being what it was, I had to make hard choices about where to focus.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos when I had good internet access and watch them later when connection was spotty. I filled notebooks with hand-drawn diagrams and summaries because that's how information sticks for me. I also used free resources I found online to supplement what I didn't fully understand from the course materials.
Q10: There were many times I studied from my handwritten notes for hours without touching the LMS at all. I'd sit with my diagrams and work through problems on paper, testing myself. Sometimes I'd study with a classmate in person, going over concepts together. None of that would show up in any log, but it was real learning happening.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach for video content. I'd watch a lecture once online, download it if possible, and then rewatch sections offline multiple times while taking visual notes. My internet isn't always reliable, so having materials saved locally was essential. The LMS would only show that initial view, not the three or four times I rewatched difficult sections.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt overwhelming and I got scared about where I was headed. Midway through the course, my stress levels peaked and I started feeling really anxious about my exam scores. Financial pressures at home made it hard to focus, and sometimes I'd log in but just stare at the screen feeling paralyzed. The mindfulness exercises they offered helped a bit, but the fear didn't fully go away.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from working or dealing with stress at home, I'd log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and other essential tasks. The fatigue made it hard to engage deeply even when I carved out time. Confusion about difficult topics sometimes made me avoid those sections entirely for a while.
Q14: Yes, honestly there were times I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about exams or money. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing what people were saying. It's like my body was going through the motions of studying but my brain wasn't fully there. Those sessions probably looked productive in the logs but weren't.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. The fear of doing them wrong and confirming I was failing made me avoid starting them. It's a weird cycle where the anxiety about the work makes you avoid the work, which creates more anxiety. I knew logically I should just start, but emotionally it was really hard to push through that scared feeling.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly my login frequency would spike. The pressure helped me focus but also increased my stress levels significantly. I wish I could have been more proactive, but the deadline pressure was often what finally pushed me to submit something, even if it wasn't my best work.
Q17: When I got feedback, it definitely affected my engagement, sometimes positively and sometimes not. Good feedback motivated me to try harder on the next assignment. But when feedback was critical or when my scores were low, it made me scared to engage further. I'd need time to process the disappointment before I could face the course materials again.
Q18: The course structure with regular video releases gave me a rhythm to follow, which helped. Having 19 online courses to manage meant I had to be strategic about where I spent my energy. The discussion components kept me connected to peers, which was motivating. But the exam-heavy assessment structure was tough because my exam scores didn't reflect how much I actually engaged with the material.
Q19: The visual elements like videos made engagement easier for someone like me. The discussion forums being active helped too. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text documents or when assignment instructions weren't clear. Also, the gap between my engagement level and my actual grades was discouraging and made me question if my approach was wrong.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat, actually. I was highly engaged, logging in frequently, watching tons of videos, participating in discussions, but my assignment completion and exam scores didn't match that activity. Sometimes you're engaging because you're trying to understand, not because you've mastered the material. High activity can also mean you're struggling and searching for help, not that you're succeeding.
Q21: I've seen classmates who barely seem to use the LMS but do okay on exams. Some people just learn differently or have access to other resources like tutors or textbooks. Maybe they're naturally good test-takers or they study efficiently offline. For me, I needed that high engagement just to stay afloat, so I can't relate to that experience personally.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and how often you access materials. They capture surface-level engagement pretty well. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement, whether you're actually learning or just clicking around anxiously. They miss offline studying, emotional state, and the real struggles happening behind the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and connect with the material, not just going through motions. It's showing up mentally even when you're scared or stressed. For me, it meant watching videos multiple times, drawing diagrams, asking questions in forums, and pushing through even when I wanted to give up. Engagement is about effort and intention, not just activity metrics.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how scared I was throughout this course, or how hard I fought to stay engaged despite that fear. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with handwritten notes or the peer study sessions. It wouldn't show that my high resilience kept me coming back even when my grades said I should give up. The emotional labor of being a student with limited resources trying to climb up, that's invisible in the data.
